articles.title: CWC meeting today: Party to finalise strategy on women’s reservation and delimitation Bills
articles.summary: The Congress Working Committee (CWC) is holding a meeting today to finalize its strategy regarding the Women's Reservation Bill and delimitation-related bills. These bills aim to reserve seats for women in legislatures and redraw electoral boundaries, respectively. The decisions taken could influence future electoral reforms and representation policies in India.

articles.title: Centre notifies CAPF Act after President’s assent
articles.summary: The Central Government has notified the Central Armed Police Forces (CAPF) Act following the President's assent. This move formalizes the legal framework governing the CAPFs. The notification is a procedural step that activates the law, impacting the functioning and authority of these security forces. The CAPF Act regulates the deployment, powers, and operational guidelines for forces like the BSF, CRPF, and CISF.

articles.title: Cabinet clears amendments to women reservation Bill
articles.summary: The Indian Cabinet has approved amendments to the Women Reservation Bill. The bill aims to reserve one-third of seats in the Lok Sabha and state legislatures for women. This move is part of efforts to enhance women's political representation in governance. The next steps involve parliamentary proceedings for the bill's passage.

articles.title: Court Cannot Substitute Its Decision In Place Of Authority's Discretion : Supreme Court Sets Aside Direction To Governor
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that courts cannot substitute their decisions for the discretion of administrative authorities, even when those authorities refuse to act. The ruling sets aside a direction issued by a court to the Governor, emphasizing that judicial intervention should not overstep executive or administrative functions. This decision underscores the separation of powers and the boundaries of judicial review in policy and governance matters.
articles.raw_summary: Even when the authority refuses to take a decision, the Court's direction should be to decide.

articles.title: Writ Against GST SCN for cancelling Registration Not Maintainable: SC
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that writ petitions challenging GST show cause notices (SCNs) for cancelling GST registration are not maintainable. The court held that such disputes must be resolved through adjudication rather than judicial review. This decision allows the GST authorities to proceed with cancellation proceedings without court interference. The ruling impacts businesses facing GST registration cancellation issues.
articles.raw_summary: The issue was whether a show cause notice cancelling GST registration can be challenged via writ. The SC held it is not maintainable and allowed adjudication to proceed. The post Writ Against GST SCN for cancelling Registration Not Maintainable: SC appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Bank Guarantee Cannot Be Invoked Without Notice or Proof of Breach: Calcutta HC
articles.summary: The Calcutta High Court ruled that a bank guarantee cannot be invoked without prior notice or proof of breach. The court held that invoking the guarantee after the defect liability period expired was arbitrary, and the absence of defects or delay invalidated the encashment action. This ruling impacts contractual and financial practices in India, particularly in cases involving government or corporate contracts.
articles.raw_summary: The Court held that invocation of the bank guarantee after expiry of the defect liability period was arbitrary. It ruled that absence of defects and delay invalidated the encashment action. The post Bank Guarantee Cannot Be Invoked Without Notice or Proof of Breach: Calcutta HC appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.summary: The Supreme Court declined to intervene in a National Company Law Appellate Tribunal (NCLAT) order that permits the Adani Group to acquire assets of the Jaypee Group. The bench directed the NCLAT to expedite its decision on a petition filed by Vedanta Limited, which had challenged the acquisition. This ruling impacts corporate governance and insolvency proceedings in India.

articles.title: Inter-play between Input Tax Credit and IBC
articles.summary: This article examines how restrictive provisions under India's Goods and Services Tax (GST) law limit the refund of unutilized Input Tax Credit (ITC) during insolvency proceedings under the Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC). It highlights conflicting judicial interpretations, creating uncertainty for creditors and liquidators. The analysis focuses on the tension between enabling clauses in GST law and restrictive provisions that may hinder the resolution process under IBC.

articles.title: Faceless Income Tax Proceedings: Evolution, Framework & Procedure
articles.summary: The Income Tax Department has implemented a faceless assessment framework to conduct tax proceedings electronically without physical interaction. This system automates case allocation and uses digital processes to enhance transparency and efficiency in tax administration. The framework covers assessments, appeals, and penalties, reducing human discretion and potential corruption. It is part of broader governance reforms to modernize tax compliance in India.

articles.title: No Reassessment on Section 80P Deduction Covered by Binding Precedent: Gujarat HC
articles.summary: The Gujarat High Court ruled that the Income Tax Department cannot reassess a taxpayer's claim for Section 80P deduction because the issue was already settled in favor of the assessee through a binding precedent. The court held that reassessment cannot be initiated on grounds already decided by a higher judicial authority. This decision reinforces legal certainty in tax assessments under Section 80P, which pertains to deductions for cooperative societies.
articles.raw_summary: The court held that reopening of assessment was invalid since the deduction issue was already settled in favour of the assessee. It ruled that reassessment cannot be based on an issue covered by binding precedent. The post No Reassessment on Section 80P Deduction Covered by Binding Precedent: Gujarat HC appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Assam Polls: ECI Bans Displaying Exit Polls From April 9 to 29
articles.summary: The Election Commission of India (ECI) has banned the display of exit polls on electronic media from April 9 to 29, citing Section 126(1)(b) of the Representation of the People Act, 1951. The ban applies during the 48 hours leading up to the conclusion of polling in the Assam elections. This is a regulatory measure to prevent the influence of opinion polls on voters during the election period.
articles.raw_summary: Further, it is clarified that as per Section 126(1)(b) of the Representation of the People Act, 1951, the display of any election matter, including the results of any opinion poll or any other poll survey, in any electronic media shall be prohibited during the period of 48 hours ending with the hour fixed for the conclusion of the poll in connection with the General Election

articles.title: Section 69 cannot be invoked when investments are recorded & taxed: ITAT Visakhapatnam
articles.summary: The Income Tax Appellate Tribunal (ITAT) in Visakhapatnam ruled that Section 69 of the Income Tax Act cannot be invoked when investments are properly recorded in financial statements and taxed. This decision impacts how tax authorities assess undisclosed income and clarifies the scope of Section 69 in tax disputes.

articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that not providing written grounds of arrest before remand violates constitutional safeguards, declaring such arrests illegal. The court granted bail in the case, emphasizing the mandatory nature of this requirement. This decision strengthens protections against arbitrary detention and upholds fundamental rights under the Constitution.

articles.title: Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) under Income Tax Act, 1961
articles.summary: The article explains that companies in India must pay Minimum Alternate Tax (MAT) if their normal tax liability is below 15% of their book profit. MAT acts as a minimum tax safeguard to ensure companies contribute a consistent amount in taxes regardless of their reported income. This provision is part of the Income Tax Act, 1961, and applies to all eligible companies.
articles.raw_summary: The framework clarifies that companies must pay MAT where normal tax liability is lower than 15% of book profit. It establishes MAT as a minimum tax safeguard, ensuring consistent tax contributions regardless of reported income. articles.title: RBI monetary policy: Call below repo to comfort banks
articles.summary: "Our attempt, of course, is to keep the WACR as near as possible to the policy rate. articles.title: Stranger Affected By Interim Order Entitled To Impleadment In Writ Proceedings : Supreme Court
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that a person not originally part of writ proceedings can be allowed to join (implead) if an interim order affects them directly. The Court emphasized that such individuals cannot be excluded solely because they were not original parties, highlighting the importance of fairness in judicial processes where interim orders have broad impacts.
articles.raw_summary: The Supreme Court has observed that a stranger to the proceedings, not being a party to the original Writ proceedings, cannot be denied impleadment if the order passed in the proceedings has a direct bearing on the stranger. “In writ proceedings, where the Court is called upon to interpret the scope and operation of an interim order already passed by it, a person who is shown to be directly and demonstrably affected by that order cannot be shut out merely because such person was not an original

articles.title: So, Here's the Problem: Textile Factories in 10 Nations Face Inconsistent Environmental Permit Rules, UN Study Finds
articles.summary: A UNEP report found that environmental permitting systems in ten major textile-producing nations, including India, have significant gaps in regulating industrial pollution from textile factories. Most national laws do not classify textile activities as environmentally significant or consider gendered impacts on workers. The study highlights inconsistencies in how spinning mills, dye houses, and garment factories are regulated, raising concerns about worker safety and environmental compliance.

articles.title: Title Suit Hit By 'Constructive Res Judicata' If Plaintiff Omitted It In Earlier Injunction Suit Where Title Was Disputed : Supreme Court
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that a plaintiff cannot file a new suit for title declaration if they omitted to seek it in an earlier injunction suit where the title was disputed. The Court held that the claim could and should have been raised in the primary suit, barring re-litigation under the principle of constructive res judicata. The bench comprising Justices clarified this legal position to prevent repetitive litigation. The ruling impacts how title disputes are handled in Indian courts.
articles.raw_summary: The Supreme Court clarified on Thursday (April 9) that a subsequent suit for declaration of title would be barred under Explanation IV to Section 11 CPC (constructive res judicata) if the plaintiff had omitted to seek title declaration in the earlier suit for permanent injunction where title was under dispute. The Court held that since the claim to title could and should have been raised in the primary suit, the party is barred from re-litigating that issue in a new suit. The bench comprising Ju

articles.title: Markets in Pieces
articles.summary: The article by Disha Joshi and Soham Gupta explores how arbitration and public policy intersect in competition law, emphasizing market coherence over individual correctness. It argues that arbitration shapes incentives by validating contracts that impact competitive conditions, even though it does not directly regulate markets. The discussion is framed within India's legal and governance systems, particularly concerning how contractual arrangements influence market behavior.
articles.raw_summary: **Disha Joshi and Soham Gupta Debates on arbitration and public policy typically focus on illegality, morality, or judicial review. This article advances a different claim. In competition law, public policy concerns market coherence rather than individual correctness. While arbitration does not regulate markets, it shapes incentives by validating contractual arrangements that affect competitive conditions. articles.title: 'Abandonment Of Service Not Voluntary Retirement', Supreme Court Denies Pensionary Benefits To SBI Clerk
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that an SBI clerk who abandoned service shortly before retirement cannot claim pensionary benefits by calling it voluntary retirement. The bench observed that the employee's prolonged absence without leave from January to December 1998 constituted abandonment, not voluntary retirement. The decision denies pensionary benefits to the appellant, setting a legal precedent for similar cases. The ruling clarifies the distinction between voluntary retirement and service abandonm
articles.raw_summary: The Supreme Court has held that an employee who abandons service shortly before retirement cannot seek pensionary benefits by claiming it to be a voluntary retirement. “…we have noticed that the present case is not of voluntary retirement, rather of voluntary abandonment of the services, wherein from 24.01.1998 to 11.12.1998, the appellant, without informing and availing leave, started remaining absent for a long time… ”, observed a bench of Justice Prashant Kumar Mishra and Justice NV Anjaria ,

articles.title: Is Blacklisting a Natural Outcome of Contract Termination? Supreme Court Clarifies
articles.summary: The Supreme Court of India has clarified that blacklisting is not an automatic consequence of contract termination. Authorities must provide notice, state reasons, and follow principles of natural justice before blacklisting any entity. This ruling impacts how government contracts are managed and disputes are resolved.

articles.title: Karnataka HC Declines to Entertain PIL Against ‘Vande Mataram’ Protocol in Schools
articles.summary: The Karnataka High Court refused to entertain a Public Interest Litigation (PIL) challenging the mandatory singing of 'Vande Mataram' in schools. The court noted that the Ministry of Home Affairs (MHA) advisory on the matter is not mandatory and lacks statutory backing. It also declined to examine the plea, citing concerns over secularism. The ruling highlights the legal and policy ambiguity surrounding the national song's status in educational institutions.
articles.raw_summary: Court says MHA advisory is not mandatory; notes national song has no statutory backing and declines to examine plea citing secularism concerns.

articles.title: Why profitable MSMEs still run out of cash
articles.summary: The article examines why many profitable Micro, Small, and Medium Enterprises (MSMEs) in India struggle with cash flow issues despite strong sales. It points to weak financial discipline, including poor tracking of cash flow and lapses in Goods and Services Tax (GST) compliance, which often leaves these businesses unable to access credit.
articles.raw_summary: Strong sales mask weak financial discipline, from poor cash flow tracking to GST lapses, leaving many small businesses locked out of credit.

articles.title: Three Vijays, two Josephs and counting: How Tamil Nadu polls have become a battle of namesakes
articles.summary: In the upcoming Tamil Nadu assembly elections on 23 April, multiple political parties are fielding candidates with identical names, creating confusion. The issue spans across the state, with parties struggling to distinguish between candidates sharing the same names. This could impact voter clarity and election administration, potentially prompting discussions on electoral reforms or candidate identification measures.
articles.raw_summary: Across the state, different parties are also facing the same problem as candidates with identical names slug it out in the high-stakes 23 April assembly election.

articles.title: Mudra loans cross Rs 40 lakh crore, Nirmala Sitharaman hails MSME credit push
articles.summary: The Pradhan Mantri Mudra Yojana has empowered millions of entrepreneurs. Over 577.9 million loans totaling ₹40.07 lakh crore have been disbursed since its 2015 launch. A significant portion of these loans went to women entrepreneurs, fostering self-reliance. The scheme has democratized access to credit, removing barriers for many individuals previously excluded from formal banking systems.

articles.title: No Abuse of Dominance Found as Price Variations Linked to Market Factors: CCI
articles.summary: The Competition Commission of India (CCI) found no abuse of dominance in the pricing of a critical railway component, attributing price variations to market factors like currency fluctuations, logistics, and quantity. The case examined allegations of inflated and discriminatory pricing. The CCI's decision concludes that the pricing changes were justified by market conditions rather than anti-competitive behavior.
articles.raw_summary: The case examined allegations of inflated and discriminatory pricing in supply of a critical railway component. The Commission held that price changes were attributable to currency fluctuations, logistics, and quantity, and found no abuse of dominance. The post No Abuse of Dominance Found as Price Variations Linked to Market Factors: CCI appeared first on TaxGuru .

articles.title: Bengaluru: Wide roads, narrow chance?
articles.summary: Bengaluru's five city corporations are reviving long-delayed road-widening projects after years of stagnation. Landowners are resisting compensation offers, and the corporations lack funds to pay cash compensations. The impasse raises questions about whether these infrastructure projects will proceed or remain stalled. The Hindu's Chetan B.C. examines the hurdles blocking progress.
articles.raw_summary: After years of failing to widen roads, the five city corporations have announced a renewed push for these projects this year. But with land losers still resisting compensation through TDR, and the corporations in no position to pay cash, will the projects take off or will the impasse continue, checks Chetan B.C.

articles.title: Sabarimala judgement proceeds on assumption that men are superior: Centre to SC

articles.title: Do All Verbal Insults Amount to Obscenity? Supreme Court Explains Limits
articles.summary: The Supreme Court of India ruled that not all verbal insults qualify as obscenity, drawing a legal distinction between offensive language and criminal obscenity. The judgment clarifies that offensive words alone do not meet the threshold for obscenity under the law. This ruling impacts free speech debates and legal interpretations of obscenity in India.
articles.raw_summary: Supreme Court draws a line between vulgarity and obscenity, ruling that offensive words alone do not meet the legal threshold.

articles.title: Government raises gas supply to fertiliser plants, boosts fuel allocation amid West Asia tensions
articles.summary: The Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas (MoPNG) has raised gas supply to fertilizer plants by 5%, reaching around 95% of their six-month average consumption starting April 9, 2026. This decision is based on available inventory and scheduled LNG cargo arrivals amid tensions in West Asia. The move aims to ensure adequate fuel supply for fertilizer production, which is critical for India's agricultural sector.

articles.title: Expert warns Middle East energy infrastructure will take months to recover
articles.summary: A ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran has raised concerns about the slow recovery of Middle East energy infrastructure, potentially keeping oil prices elevated for months. Jason Bordoff, founding director of Columbia University's Center on Global Energy Policy, warns that energy prices may not drop as hoped despite the truce. The situation could have implications for global oil supply and energy markets.
articles.raw_summary: Even with this shaky ceasefire between the U.S. and Iran, some warn the energy infrastructure in the Middle East will take months to recover, and prices may not drop as much as hoped. William Brangham discussed more with Jason Bordoff, the founding director of the Center on Global Energy Policy at Columbia University.

articles.title: From diversity to monotony, ecological communities are homogenising
articles.summary: The article explores biotic homogenisation, where diverse ecosystems worldwide are losing unique species and becoming increasingly similar due to environmental changes. This process replaces sensitive endemic species with common generalist ones, raising concerns about ecological balance and biodiversity loss. The phenomenon is documented globally but has implications for India's environmental policies and conservation strategies.
articles.raw_summary: Imagine walking through different ecosystems across the world, only to find that species inhabiting them look strikingly similar. This is the reality of biotic homogenisation, a process where diverse biological communities become increasingly similar as unique endemic species, highly sensitive to environmental changes, are replaced by common generalist species, which are insensitive to such perturbations. [ ]

articles.title: World Bank: South Asian labour market pivoting towards skills that can be amplified by AI

This trend is part of a broader shift in South Asian labour markets toward skills that can be enhanced by AI. The findings suggest potential challenges for workforce adaptation and policy interventions in regions like India.
articles.raw_summary: World Bank Group's Chief Economist Franziska Ohnsorge said that hiring has slowed down in most AI-exposed occupations or firms with large productivity gains in that industry, with job prospects in AI-exposed occupations being eroded with persistence of long-standing sub-national labour market differentials.

articles.summary: According to a study by the Wildlife Institute of India (WII), dolphins require a minimum depth of three metres in flowing rivers to survive during lean seasons. The WII study highlighted that the decreasing flow of the Chambal River has been affecting dolphin movement. During the lean season, dolphins tend to concentrate in the deeper pools of the river. The WII’s study analysed field data on the distribution of Ganges River dolphins, gharials, and other important aquatic life, including turtle

articles.title: Artificial Intelligence And Competition Law: Can Algorithms Collude Without Human Intent?
articles.summary: The article explores whether algorithms used by competing firms can collude or create cartel-like outcomes without human intent, driven by AI-driven business decisions in digital markets. It raises questions for competition law, particularly for regulators like India's CCI, as pricing, advertising, and market matching increasingly rely on automated systems. The discussion is relevant to India's evolving regulatory framework for digital markets and AI governance.

Pricing, product recommendations, advertising and matching of buyers and sellers are increasingly driven by algorithms rather than direct human decision-making. articles.title: The Sourcing Story: Industry Rewrote Its Geography Without Rewriting Its Dependence
articles.summary: The global garment industry has relocated apparel assembly to new countries over the past decade due to trade tensions and compliance pressures, but key inputs like fabrics and fibres remain concentrated in China. New research shows how production networks have reconfigured without reducing dependence on Chinese industrial inputs. This shift could impact India's role in global supply chains, given its growing manufacturing sector and trade policies. The findings underscore the need for India to
articles.raw_summary: The global garment industry has spent a decade reconfiguring its production geography in response to trade tensions, compliance pressures, and the demands of fast-moving online retail. New research now provides a detailed account of what has changed and what has not. Apparel assembly has moved, measurably and deliberately. The industrial inputs that make that assembly possible—fabrics, fibres, accessories—have stayed in China.

articles.title: Bid to clean up a rotten trail in Hyderabad’s thriving food scene
articles.summary: Beneath Hyderabad’s booming food economy lies a dark web of adulteration, mislabelling and unsafe practices. Of late, an unprecedented police-led crackdown is pulling back the curtain on a sprawling food fraud network that thrives on gaps in regulation and consumer trust. But as enforcement tightens and complaints surge, the city finds itself trying not just to fix a broken food chain, but also to restore faith in what lands on every plate, write Serish Nanisetti and Lavpreet Kaur

articles.title: The simple, spiritual living in Andhra’s Kurma Gramam village
articles.summary: Kurma Gramam, a small village in Andhra Pradesh's Hiramandalam hills, is home to 17 families living without modern amenities like electricity, internet, or LPG. The village embraces a simple, spiritually disciplined lifestyle far removed from contemporary living. The article highlights the contrast between this traditional way of life and modern conveniences.
articles.raw_summary: Nestled amidst the scenic hills of Hiramandalam in Andhra Pradesh, Kurma Gramam is a tiny hamlet, spread across 60 acres and inhabited by 17 families that stands in contrast to modern lifestyles. Cut off from the contemporary living, the village functions without electricity, mobile phones, internet connectivity or even LPG services. Life here moves at a slower pace, deeply rooted in simplicity and spiritual discipline, writes Harish Gilai

articles.title: Global sea surface temperatures are reaching record highs [Commentary]
articles.summary: Global sea surface temperatures (SST) are reaching record highs, driven by climate change. Oceans absorb most of the planet's excess heat, but the consequences of rising SST—such as marine ecosystem disruptions and extreme weather—are often overshadowed by discussions on air temperatures. This trend underscores the urgent need for climate action, including policy measures to address ocean warming and its impacts.
articles.raw_summary: Rising Sea Surface Temperatures (SST) and their seasonal fluctuations have emerged as among the most visible indicators of a warming planet. Yet, while public discourse on global warming tends to focus on rising air temperatures driven by anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions, the increase in SST and its profound consequences remain underemphasised. Oceans absorb the vast [ ]

articles.title: The three market outcome scenarios modelled by JPMorgan on the US-Iran war
articles.summary: JPMorgan has modeled three potential market outcomes based on a US-Iran war scenario. A bullish outcome would involve de-escalation in West Asia or a US Federal Reserve policy shift, leading to a broad market rally, lower bond yields, falling oil prices, tighter credit spreads, and a weaker US dollar. The scenarios are speculative and depend on geopolitical developments and central bank actions.
articles.raw_summary: A bullish scenario will be when there is proper de-escalation in West Asia or there is a policy pivot by the US Federal Reserve. In such a scenario, they see a broad "everything rally" where equities advance, bond yields drop, oil prices fall, credit spreads tighten and the US Dollar weakens.

articles.title: India resumes Iranian oil imports after 7 years as US eases sanctions
articles.summary: India has resumed importing Iranian oil for the first time in seven years after the US temporarily eased sanctions, as refiners seek to offset supply disruptions caused by tensions in the Strait of Hormuz.

articles.summary: Indian businesses are seizing opportunities after the Iran war ceasefire. Exports are resuming, and factories are returning to full capacity. Companies expect a surge in orders for reconstruction activities in war-affected nations. Pharma and packaged food sectors see increased demand.

articles.title: Trump sets ultimatum for Europe, seeks military commitments from allies in Strait of Hormuz
articles.summary: US President Donald Trump has set an ultimatum for European NATO allies, demanding concrete military commitments to secure the Strait of Hormuz. NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte conveyed this expectation to European leaders. The Strait is a critical global oil transit route, and any disruption could affect energy supplies, including India's crude oil imports. This move highlights rising tensions in the region and potential implications for international maritime security.
articles.raw_summary: NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte has informed European leaders that the US President is expecting concrete commitments for Hormuz.

articles.title: Army clears Col Purohit for Brigadier rank after 17-year legal battle
articles.summary: The Indian Army has cleared Colonel Purohit for promotion to the rank of Brigadier after a 17-year legal battle. This case underscores issues related to military governance and personnel policies.

articles.title: Mumbai’s 113-year-old Elphinstone bridge demolished, marks end of colonial-era landmark
articles.summary: Mumbai has bid farewell to the 113-year-old Elphinstone Road Overbridge, a colonial-era structure that shaped commuter movement for over a century. Its demolition clears space for new suburban rail lines and a planned double-decker corridor, marking a significant step in the city’s infrastructure upgrade.

articles.title: Amid West Asia war, FY26 goods exports may fall 2-3%: FIEO
articles.summary: India's goods exports may see a 7-8% drop in March due to the West Asia conflict. Full fiscal year 2026 exports could be 2-3% lower. However, overall goods and services exports are projected to rise 5-6% for FY26. Rising raw material prices and shipping costs are impacting shipments. Exporters seek government intervention on interest rates and policy simplification.

articles.title: The telling pause in the US-Israel war on Iran

articles.title: Israel-Iran war: India rapeseed sales shift to private traders as war lifts edible oil prices
articles.summary: Indian farmers are selling rapeseed to private traders. Market prices are higher than government rates. This is due to a rally in edible oil prices linked to the Iran war. Higher rapeseed oil supplies are limiting edible oil imports. This trend is easing reliance on costly palm oil, soy oil, and sunflower oil imports.

articles.title: Exporters' body FIEO sees 2-3 pc fall in goods exports during 2025-26
articles.summary: Indian merchandise exports are projected to decline by 2-3% in 2025-26. Global economic uncertainties, amplified by the West Asia crisis, are the primary drivers. This situation has already impacted shipments, increasing freight and raw material costs. While goods exports face a downturn, services exports are anticipated to grow. Exporters hope for stabilization following a ceasefire announcement.

articles.title: India has ample buffers to weather headwinds from Middle East conflict: World Bank
articles.summary: The World Bank stated India is well-positioned to handle the global energy shock due to strong buffers like high foreign exchange reserves and fiscal space. The strength of India's economy is reflected in its impressive GDP growth, supported by innovations in technology, solid infrastructure, and thriving internal demand.

articles.title: Tiruppur garment orders drop 15% as US, Europe demand weakens amid Iran War
articles.summary: Tiruppur's garment exports fell by 15% in March due to reduced demand from the US and Europe, where buyers held existing stock amid inflation concerns. Demand from West Asia also halted because of the Iran war, disrupting trade routes and increasing costs. Manufacturers are facing rising input prices. The situation may prompt discussions on trade policies or support measures for exporters.

articles.title: NITI Aayog lays out roadmap for horticulture transformation in J&K
articles.summary: India’s presence in global markets for apples, walnuts, almonds and saffron remains limited, with increasing import dependence and stagnant or declining exports, the Aayog said, in its report released on Wednesday. The Aayog said the mission project ‘Operation Golden Greens’ should be launched and implemented in a phased manner to improve the productivity of these commodities and make India globally competitive.

articles.title: Waiting for DeepSeek: new model to test China's AI ambitions
articles.summary: DeepSeek's next-generation AI model, codenamed 'V4,' has not been released despite reports, raising speculation about its development. The choice of computer chips for training the model—whether US-made or China-developed—could have geopolitical implications, particularly for India's AI and semiconductor policies. The delay and chip debate highlight China's push to advance its domestic AI capabilities.
articles.raw_summary: Despite reports and rumours about its imminent release, DeepSeek's next-generation "V4" model is nowhere in sight. Speculation is also swirling over the geopolitical implications of which computer chips were chosen to train and power the new system: world-leading US designs or made-in-China alternatives that the country is racing to develop.

articles.title: White Knight Corps GOC reviews security preparedness in J&K’s Rajouri
articles.summary: Lieutenant General P K Mishra, General Officer Commanding of the White Knight Corps, visited Rajouri district in Jammu and Kashmir to assess the operational readiness and security grid of the Romeo Force. The review focused on evaluating the preparedness of military units in the region. Such assessments are critical for maintaining security in areas facing ongoing challenges.
articles.raw_summary: The General Officer Commanding (GOC) of the Jammubased White Knight Corps, Lieutenant General P K Mishra, visited the Romeo Force in Jammu and Kashmir’s Rajouri district on Thursday to review the operational preparedness and the overall security grid.

articles.title: SC ends 'matrimonial battle of Mahabharata' after 80 cases, orders husband to pay ₹5-cr alimony
articles.summary: The Supreme Court ended an 80-case matrimonial dispute between a lawyer and his wife, ordering the husband to pay ₹5 crore in alimony. The court criticized the husband for exploiting legal loopholes to prolong the litigation. The ruling underscores systemic issues in family courts and the need for faster justice in gender-related disputes. The case also raises questions about legal ethics and the misuse of legal expertise in personal conflicts.
articles.raw_summary: The Supreme Court criticised the husband, who is a practicing lawyer, for using his legal knowledge to extend the cases unnecessarily.

articles.title: IGL says household gas prices unlikely to rise much despite supply pressure; awaits tax relief measures
articles.summary: Indraprastha Gas Limited (IGL) expects minimal price increases for household piped natural gas (PNG), with a potential ₹1–1.5 per SCM hike translating to about ₹20 monthly impact for households. The company is monitoring supply pressures and awaiting potential tax relief measures from the government. IGL is also cautious about CNG pricing amid similar pressures. The situation may prompt further policy support or regulatory action.

articles.title: Oil could climb back to $150 if ceasefire breaks down, warns FGE's Iman Nasseri
articles.summary: FGE's Managing Director for Middle East Research, Iman Nasseri, warns that crude oil prices could surge back to $150 per barrel if a ceasefire in a geopolitical conflict breaks down. articles.title: AI Threat For Jobs: TCS Top Management Doesn't Believe So, Says ' Hiring More Advisors'
articles.summary: TCS, India's largest IT services company, has stated that artificial intelligence (AI) will reshape rather than eliminate IT jobs. The company plans to continue hiring freshers and increase its focus on consulting and advisory roles. TCS argues that AI will boost productivity, creating new work opportunities. The statement comes amid growing concerns about AI's impact on employment in the tech sector.
articles.raw_summary: TCS says AI will reshape-not slash-IT jobs, with productivity creating more work, continued fresher hiring, and a sharper push into consulting and advisory talent.
